strategies.name: Competitive Advantage as Margin of Safety
strategies.description: The best long-term margin of safety comes not from an investment's price but from the value of a company's competitive advantage. Seek companies with strong, durable competitive moats rather than relying solely on price-based margin of safety.

strategies.name: Sustainable Growth Filter
strategies.description: Combine a discerning eye for sustainable growth with disciplined valuation over long horizons when probabilities are favorable. Look for companies with consistent positive earnings growth over extended periods.

strategies.name: Long-Term Compounding Quality
strategies.description: Own high-quality businesses for the long term to harness the power of compounding. High-quality businesses tend to have strong profitability metrics such as high ROE.

strategies.name: Quality ROE Filter
strategies.description: Select companies with high return on capital, a core building block of quality investing as emphasized in the chapter on Return on Capital.

strategies.name: Strong Balance Sheet Filter
strategies.description: Favor companies with low leverage and financial health, consistent with quality investing's emphasis on avoiding pitfalls and ensuring competitive advantage.

strategies.name: Consistent Profitability Filter
strategies.description: Screen for companies with multiple years of profitability, reflecting quality investing's focus on durable business models and recurring revenue.

strategies.name: Revenue Growth Filter
strategies.description: Identify companies with multiple sources of growth, including revenue growth, as highlighted in the Building Blocks chapter.

strategies.name: Profit Margin Quality Screen
strategies.description: Select companies with strong profit margins, indicative of pricing power, brand strength, and competitive advantage as discussed in the Patterns chapter.

strategies.name: Earnings Growth Consistency
strategies.description: Screen for companies demonstrating sustained long-term earnings growth, reflecting the quality investing emphasis on multiple sources of growth and market share gains.

strategies.name: Low Cyclicality / Recurring Revenue Proxy
strategies.description: Avoid highly cyclical companies by screening for stable earnings, consistent with the Pitfalls chapter warning about cyclicality and the Patterns chapter favoring recurring revenue.

strategies.name: Free Cash Flow Generation
strategies.description: Quality companies generate strong free cash flow, enabling good capital allocation as discussed in the Building Blocks chapter.

strategies.name: Recurring Revenue Model
strategies.description: Favor companies with significant recurring revenue streams, as exemplified by KONE's service/maintenance business model, which provides earnings stability and predictability.

strategies.name: Strong Brand / Pricing Power
strategies.description: Seek companies with demonstrated pricing power and brand strength, as illustrated by Hermès and Diageo, enabling them to raise prices without losing customers and sustaining high margins.

strategies.name: Intangible Asset Advantage
strategies.description: Identify companies with strong intangible assets (brands, patents, proprietary technology) that create barriers to entry, as exemplified by L'Oréal, Experian, and Chr. Hansen.

strategies.name: High Profitability Filter
strategies.description: Screen for companies with consistently high return on equity, indicative of durable competitive advantages seen across the quality case studies (KONE, Geberit, Hermès, etc.).

strategies.name: Low-Cost Operator Advantage
strategies.description: Identify companies with structural cost advantages over competitors, as exemplified by Ryanair's low-cost squared model, reflected in superior operating margins relative to peers.

strategies.name: Consistent Earnings Growth
strategies.description: Favor companies demonstrating sustained long-term earnings growth, as seen in quality compounders like Inditex, Novo Nordisk, and Fielmann.

strategies.name: Avoid Accounting Red Flags
strategies.description: Screen out companies showing accounting irregularities or aggressive accounting practices, as illustrated by the Elekta case study warning about earnings quality deterioration.

strategies.name: Avoid Fast-Paced Innovation Risk
strategies.description: Be cautious of companies in industries with rapid technological disruption where competitive advantages can erode quickly, as illustrated by Nokia's decline.

strategies.name: Avoid Good-Enough Goods Disruption
strategies.description: Be wary of premium-priced companies vulnerable to disruption from lower-cost 'good-enough' competitors, as illustrated by Nobel Biocare's loss of market share.

strategies.name: Avoid Boiling Frog Syndrome
strategies.description: Watch for gradual deterioration in competitive position and margins that may not be immediately obvious, as illustrated by Tesco's slow decline in quality metrics.

strategies.name: Financial Health Screen
strategies.description: Favor companies with manageable debt levels and strong balance sheets, consistent with quality investing principles across the positive case studies.

strategies.name: Market Share Gainer
strategies.description: Identify companies consistently gaining market share through superior execution and value proposition, as exemplified by Fielmann's dominance in optical retail.

strategies.name: Quality Acquirer Screen
strategies.description: Favor companies that grow through disciplined, value-accretive acquisitions in fragmented industries, as exemplified by ASSA ABLOY's quality deals strategy.

strategies.name: Cyclical Industry Caution
strategies.description: Be cautious of companies in highly cyclical industries where long business cycles can mask deteriorating fundamentals, as illustrated by Saipem's long period swells.

strategies.name: Quality Cash Generation
strategies.description: Identify companies with strong, predictable free cash flow generation as one of the three core quality characteristics

strategies.name: High Return on Capital
strategies.description: Seek companies with sustainably high returns on capital. The chapter uses 20% ROIC as an illustrative example of a quality company

strategies.name: Quality Virtuous Circle - High ROE with Growth
strategies.description: The best investments combine strong growth with high returns on capital, enabling a virtuous circle of cash generation reinvested at high rates of return. Companies should demonstrate both high returns and attractive growth opportunities.

strategies.name: Attractive Earnings Growth
strategies.description: Quality companies should demonstrate attractive growth opportunities, with positive and sustained earnings growth over time

strategies.name: Consistent Profitability
strategies.description: Quality companies generate predictable cash flows, implying consistent profitability over extended periods

strategies.name: Positive Profit Margins
strategies.description: Strong cash generation implies healthy profit margins as a baseline quality indicator

strategies.name: Quality Valuation Premium Check
strategies.description: While quality companies deserve premiums, the chapter notes these premiums are frequently insufficient relative to actual outperformance, suggesting quality companies may still be undervalued relative to their fundamental quality. Look for quality companies that are not excessively priced.

strategies.name: Consistent Organic Sales Growth
strategies.description: Look for companies with consistent organic sales growth averaging over 6% per year, with minimal years of contraction over a long period (e.g., 20 years).

strategies.name: Strong Post-Tax Return on Capital
strategies.description: Seek companies with strong post-tax return on capital in the mid-to-high teens (15-19%) or above, ideally with a gradually increasing trend over time.

strategies.name: Double-Digit Earnings Compounding
strategies.description: Target companies whose earnings growth has compounded at approximately 11% or more over long periods (e.g., 10-20 years), reflecting sustained quality.

strategies.name: Strong Cash Conversion
strategies.description: Favor companies with consistently strong cash conversion, meaning free cash flow is robust relative to net income (free cash flow at least equal to or exceeding net income).

strategies.name: Consistent Profitability Track Record
strategies.description: Select companies that have been profitable in nearly all years over a long period, reflecting resilience and quality (e.g., profitable in at least 19 out of 20 years).

strategies.name: Steadily Increasing Dividend
strategies.description: Prefer companies that pay a steadily increasing dividend, reflecting strong and sustained cash generation and shareholder-friendly capital allocation.

strategies.name: Positive Free Cash Flow Generation
strategies.description: Companies should generate positive free cash flow to fund reinvestment, dividends, and buybacks — a hallmark of quality businesses.

strategies.name: High Returns on Incremental Capital
strategies.description: Prefer companies that sustain high returns on incremental organic capital expenditures, as this yields significant compound growth. Companies should demonstrate the ability to deploy growth capex at attractive returns over extended periods.

strategies.name: Consistent Earnings Growth Through Capital Allocation
strategies.description: Look for companies that have been able to grow per-share earnings significantly over time through disciplined capital allocation, such as H&M more than doubling EPS over roughly a decade.

strategies.name: Sustained Profitability Track Record
strategies.description: Companies should have a long-term track record of generating returns, indicating efficient capital allocation across R&D, advertising, and organic growth investments.

strategies.name: Attractive Acquisition Pricing (Bolt-on M&A)
strategies.description: Companies that make acquisitions should do so at attractive valuations, such as Essilor purchasing companies at six to seven times cash flow. As a proxy, the acquiring company itself should trade at reasonable valuations reflecting disciplined capital deployment.

strategies.name: Organic Revenue Growth Supplemented by Acquisitions
strategies.description: Favor companies that can generate consistent revenue growth through a combination of organic growth and disciplined bolt-on acquisitions, such as adding more than 3% annual sales growth from acquisitions alone.

strategies.name: Positive Operating Margins Indicating Investment Efficiency
strategies.description: Companies that effectively allocate capital to R&D, advertising, and growth capex should demonstrate healthy operating margins, reflecting that these investments translate into profitable operations rather than value destruction.

strategies.name: Avoid Value-Destroying Acquisitive Companies
strategies.description: Acquisitions are a common source of value destruction. Prefer companies that prioritize organic growth over M&A, or that have a proven track record of disciplined, small bolt-on acquisitions rather than large transformative deals. High debt-to-equity may signal aggressive acquisition-funded leverage.

strategies.name: Consistent EPS Growth (Quality Compounder)
strategies.description: Favor companies that demonstrate consistent per-share earnings growth over time, reflecting disciplined capital allocation that compounds shareholder value, as exemplified by H&M's doubling of EPS.

strategies.name: Quality ROIC High-Teens Threshold
strategies.description: Quality investing focuses on companies able to invest capital at high rates of return, specifically post-tax returns on capital in the high-teens (around 17-19%) or higher.

strategies.name: Sustained High Return on Capital
strategies.description: Focus on companies where return on capital has been high and stable over time, as these companies are outliers able to buck the statistical trend of mean reversion and sustain superior returns over the long term.

strategies.name: Avoid Leverage-Inflated ROE
strategies.description: High return on equity driven by excessive debt is misleading and risky, as demonstrated by failed financial institutions in 2008. Companies should generate high returns without relying on excessive leverage.

strategies.name: High Profit Margin as Competitive Advantage
strategies.description: Gross profit margin demonstrates competitive advantage and is the purest expression of a company's pricing power and brand value. Companies with high profit margins relative to competitors possess a 'Midas touch' from brand, IP, or other advantages.

strategies.name: Positive Economic Profit Screen
strategies.description: Companies generating economic profit (returns on capital exceeding the opportunity cost of capital) possess competitive advantages that protect returns from competition.

strategies.name: Strong Cash Conversion Quality
strategies.description: Cash conversion reflects a company's working capital intensity and the conservatism of its accounting policies. Companies with strong free cash flow relative to net income demonstrate quality cash conversion.

strategies.name: Consistent Profitability Over Time
strategies.description: Quality companies should demonstrate consistent profitability over extended periods, as the chapter emphasizes returns that are high and stable over time rather than cyclical spikes.

strategies.name: Quality Growth Rate Target
strategies.description: Focus on companies likely to deliver reliable long-term growth of 7.5-10% annually (half to two-thirds of the 15%+ that aggressive growth investors seek), prioritizing reliability over speed of growth.

strategies.name: Positive Revenue Growth Filter
strategies.description: Invest in companies operating in growing end markets rather than shrinking ones. Companies in shrinking markets face destructive competition through price discounts and promotions.

strategies.name: High Return on Capital with Growth
strategies.description: Opportunities for growth maximize the benefits derived from high returns on capital. Seek companies with strong ROE combined with positive revenue growth, as growth amplifies the compounding effect of high returns.

strategies.name: Consistent Earnings Growth Track Record
strategies.description: Companies with a proven track record of steady growth (such as consistent market share gains) are highly attractive investments. Look for companies with positive long-term earnings growth over 10 years and consistent profitability.

strategies.name: Avoid Frenetic Growth Stocks
strategies.description: Avoid companies growing sales at extremely high rates (15%+ annually) as these are less likely to be reliable and sustainable. Quality investors prefer moderate, dependable growth.

strategies.name: Sustained Profitability Filter
strategies.description: Quality companies should demonstrate sustained profitability over many years, indicating they operate in favorable end markets and have durable competitive advantages. Look for companies profitable in most of the last 10 years.

strategies.name: Positive Operating Margin Filter
strategies.description: Companies in growing markets with competitive advantages should maintain healthy operating margins, avoiding the margin-destructive price discounting seen in shrinking markets.

strategies.name: Prudent Balance Sheet Filter
strategies.description: Exceptional managers minimize borrowing and maintain a prudent balance sheet, enabling counter-cyclical investment. Companies with low debt-to-equity ratios reflect disciplined capital stewardship.

strategies.name: Strong Interest Coverage
strategies.description: Companies that minimize borrowing should have strong interest coverage ratios, indicating they can easily service any debt they do carry, consistent with the chapter's emphasis on prudent balance sheets.

strategies.name: Positive Profit Margin Filter
strategies.description: Companies led by good managers who relentlessly pursue improvement and eradicate threats should demonstrate healthy profit margins, reflecting operational excellence.

strategies.name: Organic Growth Evidence
strategies.description: Good managers invest in organic growth rather than value-destructive acquisitions. Positive revenue growth rates suggest organic expansion and long-term vision.

strategies.name: Strong ROE - Capital Stewardship
strategies.description: Disciplined stewards of shareholder capital should generate strong returns on equity, reflecting effective deployment of shareholder funds without excessive leverage.

strategies.name: Positive Free Cash Flow
strategies.description: Good managers who are disciplined capital allocators and avoid value-destructive acquisitions should generate positive and meaningful free cash flow relative to market cap.

strategies.name: Long-Term Earnings Growth
strategies.description: Managers with long-term vision and tenacity to realize it should produce sustained earnings growth over a decade, reflecting the compounding benefits of disciplined stewardship.

strategies.name: Mini-Monopoly High Profit Margin
strategies.description: Companies with mini-monopoly characteristics (unique customer benefits, high brand loyalty, limited real customer choice) tend to exhibit very high profit margins. Screen for companies with exceptionally high profit margins as an indicator of monopoly-like pricing power.

strategies.name: Partial Monopoly EBITDA Margin Screen
strategies.description: Companies with partial monopoly characteristics (localized dominance, switching costs) can achieve EBITDA margins comfortably over 50%, as illustrated by Ambev in Brazil. Screen for companies with very high operating margins as a proxy for EBITDA margins indicating broken competition.

strategies.name: Sustained High Returns on Capital
strategies.description: Companies like Atlas Copco that benefit from partial monopolies through switching costs and back-end revenue sustain high returns on capital over many years. Screen for companies with consistently high ROE as evidence of durable competitive advantage from industry structure.

strategies.name: Strong Operating Margins with Cyclical Resilience
strategies.description: Companies with partial monopoly characteristics (like compressor market leaders) maintain strong operating margins even in cyclical end markets. Screen for companies with solid operating margins combined with positive profitability as evidence of structural competitive advantage.

strategies.name: Recurring Revenue / Switching Cost Indicator
strategies.description: Companies with high switching costs generate recurring back-end revenue (spare parts, upgrades, maintenance contracts) at high margins relative to production cost. High and stable profit margins combined with consistent revenue growth suggest recurring revenue streams from switching costs.

strategies.name: High Gross Margin Quality
strategies.description: Companies with high gross margins (exceeding 70%) indicate strong pricing power, often driven by intangible customer benefits such as brand loyalty, image, and emotional connection. L'Oréal is cited as an example with gross margins exceeding 70%.

strategies.name: Sustained Dividend Growth (50+ Years)
strategies.description: Quality companies with strong intangible customer benefits demonstrate sustained dividend increases over very long periods (50+ years), indicating durable competitive advantages and consistent cash generation.

strategies.name: Strong Dividend Growth Rate
strategies.description: Quality companies with pricing power and intangible benefits can compound dividend growth at high rates. L'Oréal achieved 16% compounded annual dividend growth over 14 years, suggesting screening for companies with strong dividend growth trajectories.

strategies.name: Pricing Power via High Gross Margins
strategies.description: Companies whose products confer intangible, assurance, or convenience benefits to customers tend to have pricing power, reflected in gross margins exceeding 70%. Combined with strong cash generation and good returns on capital, these companies sustain market leadership.

strategies.name: Steady Revenue Growth Trajectory
strategies.description: Quality companies with strong customer benefits demonstrate steady top-line growth over time, reflecting resilient demand even during economic contractions. Screen for companies with positive and consistent revenue growth.

strategies.name: Good Returns on Capital
strategies.description: Companies with strong customer benefits (intangible, assurance, convenience) generate good returns on capital, forming a virtuous circle with high margins and strong cash generation.

strategies.name: Strong Cash Generation Quality Screen
strategies.description: Quality companies with durable customer benefits generate strong free cash flow, enabling reinvestment and sustained dividend growth. Screen for positive and substantial free cash flow relative to market cap.

strategies.name: Consistent Profitability Over Decade
strategies.description: Companies whose products are considered non-discretionary due to intangible or assurance benefits (e.g., cosmetics, essential consumer products) tend to remain profitable even during economic contractions. Screen for companies profitable in at least 10 consecutive years.

strategies.name: Sustainable Competitive Advantage via R&D Scale
strategies.description: Favor companies that outspend rivals on R&D and have scale advantages that create barriers to entry, including technological complexity, advanced research skills, and high capital costs for research equipment. Companies should demonstrate sustained technological leadership over long periods rather than fleeting advantages.

strategies.name: Replicable Competitive Advantage Filter
strategies.description: Seek companies whose competitive advantages are scalable and replicable across new markets, rather than being tied to a single location or non-scalable asset. The advantage should be transferable to new geographies or product lines.

strategies.name: Incremental Innovation Leader
strategies.description: Prefer long-established industry leaders in complex product categories where innovation is incremental rather than revolutionary. These companies benefit from deep product knowledge and cumulative gains that entrench their position and deter new entrants.

strategies.name: Data-Driven Competitive Moat
strategies.description: Favor companies that collect and leverage proprietary data to continuously improve their products and services, creating a self-reinforcing competitive advantage (e.g., user data improving algorithms, credit-scoring models updated with new data).

strategies.name: Diversified Innovation Portfolio
strategies.description: Prefer companies with a diverse set of innovation opportunities across multiple product lines, mitigating the risk of total disruption. Losing one technology race should not threaten the entire business.

strategies.name: Network Effects Moat
strategies.description: Seek companies that benefit from network effects where the value of the product or service increases as more users adopt it, creating a self-reinforcing competitive advantage.

strategies.name: Distribution Advantage
strategies.description: Favor companies with distribution advantages that are difficult for competitors to replicate, providing a durable competitive edge in reaching customers.

strategies.name: Above-Average Economic Returns
strategies.description: Screen for companies delivering above-average economic returns as evidence of a competitive advantage. Use ROE as a proxy for superior economic returns relative to the market average.

strategies.name: Market Leader Position
strategies.description: Prefer companies that are global market leaders or among the top three players in their industry, as market leadership combined with scale creates barriers to entry and sustains competitive advantage.

strategies.name: Long Product Lead Time Advantage
strategies.description: Favor companies in industries with long product development lead times where incumbents can use incremental innovation to close any gap created by new entrants before those entrants can reach market, creating a natural barrier to competition.

strategies.name: Strong Cash Generation
strategies.description: Seek companies with strong, predictable cash generation as a hallmark of quality

strategies.name: Sustainably High Returns on Capital
strategies.description: Quality companies exhibit sustainably high returns on capital, indicating efficient use of invested resources

strategies.name: Attractive Growth Opportunities
strategies.description: Quality companies should demonstrate attractive growth, evidenced by positive revenue and earnings growth

strategies.name: Predictable Cash Generation
strategies.description: Companies with predictable, recurring cash flows are preferred — implied by consistent profitability over multiple years

strategies.name: Recurring Revenue Stability Filter
strategies.description: Favor companies with high degrees of recurring revenues, which increase business stability and cash flow predictability. Companies with large installed bases of equipment or software that generate ongoing service, maintenance, or licensing revenue streams are preferred.

strategies.name: Revenue Stability Through Cycles
strategies.description: Seek companies whose recurring revenue streams (e.g., service contracts on installed equipment) provide stable revenues even during economic downturns, making revenue growth relatively predictable despite operating in cyclical industries.

strategies.name: Positive Profit Margin on Upfront Sales
strategies.description: Ensure that companies with recurring revenue models are not generating new upfront sales at poor returns. If upfront sales costs eat into recurring revenue gains, the business model is weakened. Companies should demonstrate positive operating margins indicating the upfront business is not destroying value.

strategies.name: Dual Competitive Advantage Filter
strategies.description: Prefer companies that enjoy competitive advantages on both the front-end (upfront product sales) and the back-end (recurring revenue), as the economics of such businesses are 'doubly strong.' This is reflected in strong profit margins and high returns on equity.

strategies.name: Consistent Revenue Growth Filter
strategies.description: Companies with large installed bases and recurring revenue models should exhibit relatively predictable and positive revenue growth over time, reflecting the virtuous circle of growing installed base and expanding service revenues.

strategies.name: Sustained Profitability Filter
strategies.description: Companies with strong recurring revenue models should demonstrate consistent profitability over many years, as the predictable nature of their revenue streams supports sustained earnings. Look for companies profitable in most or all of the last 10 years.

strategies.name: Positive Free Cash Flow Filter
strategies.description: Recurring revenue businesses should generate predictable and positive free cash flows, as the stability of their revenue streams translates into reliable cash generation.

strategies.name: Friendly Middleman Business Model
strategies.description: Invest in companies whose products are selected by expert middlemen (e.g., doctors, plumbers, optometrists) while a different party (the end consumer) pays the bill, enabling premium pricing power and margin protection.

strategies.name: High Operating Margin from Pricing Power
strategies.description: Companies benefiting from friendly middlemen dynamics tend to have high operating margins because the decision-maker (middleman) is insensitive to price while the end customer pays. Look for companies with above-average operating margins indicating pricing power.

strategies.name: Strong Return on Equity - Quality Business
strategies.description: Companies with product differentiation, professional lock-in, and expert middleman endorsement tend to generate sustainably high returns on equity, indicating a quality franchise with competitive advantages.

strategies.name: Revenue Growth from Lock-In Effects
strategies.description: Companies that lock in middlemen through training, product familiarity, and switching costs should exhibit steady revenue growth as professionals continue recommending their products.

strategies.name: Toll Road Niche Supplier
strategies.description: Identify companies that supply products or services representing a small fraction of their customers' cost base but are crucial to successful operations, creating significant barriers to entry and oligopolistic market structures.

strategies.name: Toll Road Pricing Power - High Margins
strategies.description: Toll road companies can charge significant fees relative to their costs because their products/services are essential but represent immaterial costs to customers. This should manifest as high operating and profit margins.

strategies.name: Toll Road Revenue Stability
strategies.description: Toll road businesses extract economics from every unit of volume in larger, relatively stable industries, suggesting consistent revenue growth and profitability over long periods.

strategies.name: Oligopolistic Market Position - High ROE
strategies.description: Companies operating in oligopolistic, stable competitive landscapes with high barriers to entry should demonstrate sustainably high returns on equity, reflecting their entrenched market position and pricing power.

strategies.name: Low Cost High Value Ingredient Supplier
strategies.description: Magic ingredient companies supply inputs that are low cost to the customer but high value in production processes. These businesses should show strong profitability and consistent earnings due to non-discretionary demand.

strategies.name: Consistent Earnings Growth - Entrenched Supplier
strategies.description: Toll road companies benefit from stable, recurring demand tied to larger industry volumes. Look for companies with positive long-term earnings growth reflecting their entrenched positions.

strategies.name: Low-Price Plus Brand Moat
strategies.description: Identify companies that combine a low-price strategy with brand recognition for thrift and product differentiation, creating a durable competitive advantage that pure low-cost competitors cannot easily replicate. Look for companies where the brand itself is synonymous with affordable quality.

strategies.name: Scale-Dependent Low-Cost Operator
strategies.description: Favor large-scale companies pursuing low-price strategies, as scale is described as essential for sustaining the low-price-plus model through complex supply chain coordination, rapid demand response, and distribution efficiency. Larger market cap serves as a proxy for the scale advantages described.

strategies.name: Low-Cost Squared Operating Margin
strategies.description: Companies following a 'low-cost squared' strategy achieve competitive advantages through cumulative cost savings across every process. Look for companies with healthy operating margins despite low pricing, indicating deep structural cost advantages that rivals cannot easily replicate.

strategies.name: Sustainable Low-Price Profitability
strategies.description: Quality low-price companies should demonstrate consistent profitability over many years, proving the durability of their cost advantage model. Companies that are profitable most years despite low-price strategies have proven sustainable competitive advantages.

strategies.name: Low-Price Plus Revenue Growth
strategies.description: Successful low-price-plus companies like IKEA, Inditex, and Costco have experienced 'phenomenal success' with strong growth. Look for companies with positive revenue growth as evidence that their low-price model is gaining market share and deterring competitors.

strategies.name: Cost Leadership with Strong ROE
strategies.description: Companies that achieve low-cost squared advantages should generate strong returns on equity despite thin margins, as their cumulative cost savings and scale translate into efficient capital deployment.

strategies.name: Stable High Gross Margins (Pricing Power Indicator)
strategies.description: Companies with genuine pricing power should exhibit stable high gross margins, as the ability to raise prices above cost inflation preserves and expands margins over time. Look for companies with consistently high profit margins as evidence of pricing power.

strategies.name: Expanding Gross Margin Over Time
strategies.description: Pricing power should manifest as incremental periodic expansion in gross margin. Companies that can raise prices above cost inflation will show improving margins over time.

strategies.name: High Operating Margin (Hermès-Level Benchmark)
strategies.description: Companies with exceptional pricing power demonstrate operating margins exceeding 30%, as illustrated by the Hermès case study. Screen for companies with very high operating margins as candidates with strong pricing power.

strategies.name: High Return on Invested Capital (Pricing Power Benefit)
strategies.description: Pricing power requires no capital expenditure to raise prices, resulting in enhanced returns on capital. Companies with ROIC exceeding 30% demonstrate the capital-light benefits of pricing power, as exemplified by Hermès.

strategies.name: Consistent Revenue Growth (Pricing Power Growth)
strategies.description: Companies with genuine pricing power can achieve consistent top-line growth even in difficult environments. Hermès achieved an 11% CAGR over two decades with the worst year being 4% growth. Screen for companies with positive and consistent revenue growth.

strategies.name: Strong Cash Flow Generation
strategies.description: Companies with pricing power generate strong and predictable cash flows due to their ability to consistently raise prices. Screen for companies with positive free cash flow as an indicator of pricing power sustainability.

strategies.name: Composite Pricing Power Screen
strategies.description: Combine high operating margins, high returns on equity, and consistent revenue growth to identify companies likely possessing pricing power. Based on the chapter's description of pricing power manifesting in high margins, high ROIC, and steady growth.
strategies.expression: operating_margin > 0.25 and roe > 0.25 and revenue_growth_rate > 0.04 and profit_margin > 0.15

strategies.name: Consistent Profitability (Pricing Power Durability)
strategies.description: Companies with durable pricing power should be profitable across economic cycles, reflecting their ability to maintain prices even in downturns. Screen for companies that have been profitable in most or all recent years.

strategies.name: Brand Premium Pricing Power
strategies.description: Invest in companies with strong, differentiated brands that enable premium pricing, evidenced by above-average profit margins. Strong brands create customer attachment (emotional or logical) and allow companies to charge more than competitors.

strategies.name: Market Share Leadership Filter
strategies.description: Favor companies that are market leaders in their category, as exemplified by Diageo's ~30% average market share and ~50% in premium Scotch. Market leaders with strong brands tend to have durable competitive advantages.

strategies.name: Consistent Revenue Growth from Pricing Power
strategies.description: Look for companies demonstrating consistent revenue growth driven by pricing power rather than just volume. Diageo delivered 6% compound annual revenue growth per case, indicating strong brand-driven pricing power.

strategies.name: High Barriers to Entry - Capital Intensive Heritage
strategies.description: Invest in companies operating in industries with high barriers to entry (e.g., time-based aging requirements, non-replicable heritage, geographic restrictions) that protect competitive position and pricing power.

strategies.name: Avoid Undifferentiated Brand Industries
strategies.description: Avoid companies in structurally challenging industries (airlines, banking, telecom, newspapers) where even strong brand recognition does not translate to superior financial performance due to lack of differentiation and customer attachment.

strategies.name: Strong Profitability from Brand Equity
strategies.description: Companies with deep heritage brands and savvy marketing that drives brand equity should demonstrate strong profitability. Look for companies with high return on equity as evidence of brand-driven competitive advantage.

strategies.name: Enduring Brand - Long Operating History
strategies.description: Prefer companies with brands that have endured over time, as heritage brands are impossible to replicate. Companies with long track records of profitability signal durable brand strength.

strategies.name: Large Cap Brand Leaders
strategies.description: Focus on larger companies that can sustain significant long-term brand investment (e.g., Diageo spends ~$2.5B annually on advertising), which would not be economic for smaller brands. Scale in brand investment creates a self-reinforcing competitive advantage.

strategies.name: High Gross Margin for Innovation Investment
strategies.description: Companies with high gross margins have more resources to invest in R&D, advertising, and distribution, creating a virtuous cycle of growth. High gross margins indicate the ability to outspend rivals on innovation.

strategies.name: Profitable Innovation - Margin Stability
strategies.description: Innovation must be profitable to be attractive. Companies where innovation comes alongside declining margins are engaged in costly cannibalization, not value creation. Look for companies with stable or expanding operating margins alongside R&D spending.

strategies.name: Sustained Revenue Growth from Innovation
strategies.description: Innovation dominance facilitates both volume growth and pricing power. Companies with consistent positive revenue growth demonstrate that their innovation efforts are translating into commercial success.

strategies.name: High ROE from Innovation-Driven Competitive Advantage
strategies.description: Companies with innovation dominance forge a virtuous cycle of growth that should manifest in superior returns on equity, reflecting the value creation from their R&D investments.

strategies.name: Avoid Investor Exuberance on Breakthrough Expectations
strategies.description: Widespread anticipation of big breakthroughs often yields investor exuberance that suppresses investment returns. Avoid companies trading at extreme valuations driven by speculative breakthrough expectations.

strategies.name: Incremental Innovation Revenue Growth
strategies.description: Incremental innovation generally produces more predictable revenue growth than reliance on big breakthroughs. Companies with steady, consistent earnings growth over a decade demonstrate this pattern.

strategies.name: High Franchise EBIT Margin
strategies.description: Franchise-based businesses with very high EBIT margins in their franchise divisions indicate powerful brand economics and capital-light growth. InterContinental Hotels Group's franchise division exceeds 80% EBIT margins as a benchmark.

strategies.name: Capital-Light Forward Integrator (High ROE)
strategies.description: Forward integrators with franchise or licensing models require modest incremental capital for expansion, resulting in theoretically very high returns on capital. Look for companies with strong ROE as a proxy for capital-light, brand-powered business models.

strategies.name: Revenue Stability / Positive Profit Margin
strategies.description: Franchise fee models based on revenue (not net income) add predictability. Companies with consistently positive profit margins across cycles indicate stable, franchise-like economics.

strategies.name: Revenue Growth from Forward Integration
strategies.description: Successful forward integrators (own-store expansion, online selling, franchising) should demonstrate positive revenue growth as they expand their direct-to-consumer channels and add franchisees.

strategies.name: Low Debt for Franchise/Brand-Powered Models
strategies.description: Capital-light franchise models where franchisees provide most assets should result in lower debt levels for the franchisor, as expansion does not require heavy capital investment.

strategies.name: Large Cap Global Brand Scale
strategies.description: The most successful forward integrators are powerful global brands (LVMH, Nike, Marriott, Apple, Nestlé). A minimum scale requirement exists to support franchise systems, A&P spending, and logistics infrastructure. Large market cap serves as a proxy for global brand scale.

strategies.name: Strong Operating Margin (Brand Power Indicator)
strategies.description: Companies with strong operating margins indicate brand pricing power — the ability to name their own prices rather than ceding control to distributors or retailers. This is a hallmark of successful forward integrators.

strategies.name: Consistent Earnings Growth (Franchise Scalability)
strategies.description: Once critical mass is established in a franchise model, incremental profit contribution from additional franchised revenue is substantial, leading to sustained earnings growth over time.

strategies.name: Market Share Gainer - Revenue Growth Above Industry
strategies.description: Prefer companies that consistently gain market share, evidenced by revenue growth rates exceeding the overall industry/market growth rate. Companies with better products and superior execution should regularly attract new customers from competitors.

strategies.name: Consistent Market Share Gains - Long-Term EPS Growth
strategies.description: Market share gainers drive attractive long-term compound earnings growth. Fielmann example shows EPS tripling over an extended period, implying sustained double-digit or high single-digit EPS growth over 10+ years as a hallmark of quality market share gainers.

strategies.name: Healthy Operating Margins Despite Competitive Pricing
strategies.description: Quality market share gainers maintain strong operating margins even while offering competitive pricing, due to scale and cost advantages. Fielmann's margins approach 20%, suggesting quality companies should have robust profitability despite competitive pricing.

strategies.name: Avoid Market Share Gained Through Margin Sacrifice
strategies.description: Be wary of companies sacrificing price and gross margins for the sake of market share. If a company's profit margins are declining while revenue grows, this may indicate unsustainable share gains driven by price cuts rather than genuine product superiority.

strategies.name: Scale-Driven Competitive Advantage - Large Market Cap
strategies.description: Market share gains reinforce competitive advantages based on scale. The largest companies among competitors command larger budgets for R&D, advertising, and distribution. Prefer companies that are among the largest in their competitive set.

strategies.name: Strong ROE from Scale Advantages
strategies.description: Companies with dominant market share and scale-driven cost advantages should generate superior returns on equity, reflecting efficient capital deployment and competitive moats.

strategies.name: Low Debt for Sustainable Market Share Growth
strategies.description: Quality companies gaining market share organically (through better products and execution rather than debt-fueled acquisitions) should maintain healthy balance sheets.

strategies.name: Steady Revenue Growth - Market Share Proxy
strategies.description: Companies gaining 1-2% market share annually in mature markets (like Fielmann in German eyewear) should show consistent mid-to-high single digit revenue growth even in slow-growth industries.

strategies.name: Global Revenue Diversification
strategies.description: Seek companies that generate a significant majority of their revenue outside their home market, indicating successful global expansion and reduced dependence on any single domestic market. The Inditex example shows 81% of sales outside Spain as a benchmark for strong global capabilities.

strategies.name: Sustained Revenue Growth from Global Expansion
strategies.description: Companies with global capabilities and industry leadership tend to deliver more sustainable growth than geographically constrained peers. Look for companies with consistent positive revenue growth rates as evidence of successful global expansion.

strategies.name: Large Scale Global Operator
strategies.description: Favor companies with significant scale as measured by market capitalization and revenue, as global industry leadership requires substantial operational footprint. The chapter emphasizes product differentiation and business model over pure scale, but scale is a byproduct of successful global expansion.

strategies.name: Consistent Profitability of Global Leaders
strategies.description: Global industry leaders with differentiated products and adaptable business models should demonstrate sustained profitability over many years, reflecting their competitive advantages across multiple markets.

strategies.name: Strong Operating Margins from Differentiated Business Model
strategies.description: Companies with true global capabilities and product differentiation (like Inditex's demand-pull model or Rolls-Royce's engineering excellence) should exhibit above-average operating margins, reflecting pricing power and operational efficiency across markets.

strategies.name: Positive Earnings Growth Trajectory
strategies.description: Global leaders with the ability to expand into new markets should show positive long-term earnings growth, as geographic expansion provides a runway for sustained growth that domestically constrained companies cannot match.

strategies.name: Avoid Overleveraged Global Expanders
strategies.description: Companies that expand globally through debt-fueled acquisitions (like Tesco's failed global expansion) carry significant risk. Favor companies with manageable debt levels that fund expansion through operational cash flows.

strategies.name: Return on Capital Focus
strategies.description: Prefer companies that prioritize return on capital over short-term earnings growth, indicating a long-term oriented corporate culture. Companies using return on capital as a performance metric and for incentive compensation are favored.

strategies.name: Organic Growth over Acquisitions
strategies.description: Prefer companies that allocate capital to organic capex, R&D, and advertising to drive long-term growth rather than relying on aggressive acquisition campaigns for revenue growth.

strategies.name: Conservative Leverage (Family Ownership Style)
strategies.description: Durable family-owned firms typically avoid excessive leverage and use retained earnings to grow. Favor companies with low debt-to-equity ratios reflecting conservative financial management.

strategies.name: Long-Term Sustainable Growth
strategies.description: Look for companies with a long-term vision focused on sustainable growth and return on capital, evidenced by consistent revenue and earnings growth over extended periods rather than short-term spikes.

strategies.name: Consistent Profitability (Execution Quality)
strategies.description: Companies that execute well tend to be less 'accident'-prone and deliver on promises consistently. Look for companies with sustained profitability over many years as a proxy for execution quality.

strategies.name: Avoid Excessive Equity Dilution
strategies.description: Favor companies that use retained earnings rather than serial equity offerings to grow, reflecting disciplined capital allocation typical of quality corporate cultures.

strategies.name: Steady Earnings Pattern (Low Volatility)
strategies.description: Companies with strong execution cultures tend to show a pattern of steady small adjustments rather than bold reorganizations, reflected in consistent earnings without sudden large write-downs or cost overruns.

strategies.name: High and Growing Operating Margins
strategies.description: Look for companies with operating margins that have been growing over a decade, indicating durable competitive advantages and cost-to-replicate barriers. Experian's margins grew from 21% to 27% over a decade, suggesting a threshold of at least 20% operating margin with an upward trend.

strategies.name: Sustained Organic Revenue Growth
strategies.description: Companies with durable competitive advantages that are costly to replicate should demonstrate consistent organic revenue growth. Experian achieved a 10-year organic sales CAGR of 6%, suggesting screening for companies with steady revenue growth rates.

strategies.name: Consistent Long-Term Profitability
strategies.description: Companies with high cost-to-replicate advantages should show sustained profitability over many years, resisting mean reversion. Screen for companies that have been profitable for at least 10 consecutive years.

strategies.name: High ROE from Irreplaceable Assets
strategies.description: Companies with assets that are extremely costly to replicate (databases, network effects, decades of accumulated data/IP) tend to generate above-average returns on equity. Screen for companies with strong ROE indicating durable competitive positioning.

strategies.name: Low Marginal Cost Business Model
strategies.description: Companies whose products can be sold multiple times with near-zero marginal cost (like information/data businesses) tend to have high profit margins. Screen for companies with high profit margins as a proxy for low marginal cost structures.

strategies.name: Mean Reversion Resistance - Earnings Growth
strategies.description: Quality companies with high cost-to-replicate advantages can sustain above-average earnings growth over long periods, resisting mean reversion. Screen for companies with positive 10-year earnings growth as evidence of durable advantage.

strategies.name: Avoid Cyclical Earnings Volatility
strategies.description: Be cautious of companies whose strong current earnings and returns are driven by cyclical factors rather than sustainable competitive advantages. Cyclical companies may appear to have high quality metrics at the peak of their cycle but face significant downside when the cycle turns.

strategies.name: Consistent Profitability Over Full Cycle
strategies.description: Favor companies that have demonstrated profitability across multiple years (covering full economic cycles) rather than those with only recent strong performance, to filter out cyclically-inflated results.

strategies.name: Stable Earnings Growth Filter
strategies.description: Look for companies with positive long-term earnings growth that is not solely dependent on a recent cyclical upswing. Comparing 10-year average EPS to 3-year average EPS can help identify whether recent performance is an anomaly or part of a sustainable trend.
strategies.expression: avg_eps_10y > 0 and avg_eps_3y > 0 and avg_eps_3y <= avg_eps_10y * 2

strategies.name: Strong Cash Generation
strategies.description: Quality companies are characterized by attractive cash generation. Favor companies with positive free cash flow as a sign of genuine economic value creation rather than accounting-driven earnings.

strategies.name: High Return on Equity
strategies.description: Quality investing emphasizes high returns on capital as a core characteristic. Companies should demonstrate strong ROE as evidence of durable competitive advantages, though this must be scrutinized for cyclical or transient factors.

strategies.name: Positive Revenue Growth
strategies.description: Growth is identified as one of the three attractive economic characteristics of quality companies. Companies should show positive revenue growth, but investors must assess whether growth is sustainable or driven by temporary tailwinds.

strategies.name: Avoid Deeply Cyclical Commoditized Industries
strategies.description: Minimize exposure to deeply cyclical industries such as energy and mining where companies sell commoditized products, as they rarely command sustainable competitive advantages. Supply-demand industries like steel making or offshore drilling are least attractive due to unpredictable price paths and overcapacity risks.

strategies.name: Favor Companies with Stable Revenue (Low Cyclicality)
strategies.description: Prefer companies where revenue fluctuations are small enough that they barely register, indicating resilience to economic cycles. Companies with more stable, predictable cash flow patterns are preferred over those with tidal vicissitudes in demand.

strategies.name: Favor Flow Product Businesses Over CapEx-Dependent Businesses
strategies.description: Prefer companies whose revenue is tied to customers' operating costs (flow products like spare parts, maintenance, testing services) rather than customers' capital expenditure. Flow product companies maintain more predictable revenue even during cyclical downturns because production and maintenance continue even when new capital projects are postponed.

strategies.name: Consistent Profitability Through Cycles
strategies.description: Seek companies that remain profitable through economic cycles, demonstrating the ability to grow through-cycle using sustainable competitive advantages. Companies should show profitability across multiple years including downturns.

strategies.name: Positive Through-Cycle Earnings Growth
strategies.description: Quality companies in cyclical industries should demonstrate the ability to grow earnings over a full economic cycle (approximately 10 years), using periods of disruption to widen their competitive lead through counter-cyclical investment.

strategies.name: Avoid Highly Leveraged Cyclical Companies
strategies.description: Companies in cyclical industries with significant capital outlays and debt are vulnerable during downturns when demand drops and overcapacity costs come due. Prefer companies with manageable debt levels that can invest counter-cyclically.

strategies.name: Sustainable Competitive Advantage (High Operating Margin)
strategies.description: Companies with differentiated or branded products that command competitive advantages tend to maintain higher margins through cycles, unlike commoditized producers. Higher operating margins suggest pricing power and differentiation.

strategies.name: Stable Revenue Base (Moderate Revenue Growth Consistency)
strategies.description: Prefer companies that show positive revenue growth even through cycles, indicating their business model is not purely dependent on supply-demand dynamics of commoditized products.

strategies.name: Product Durability Test (10-Year Relevance)
strategies.description: Invest only in companies whose products will still exist in a similar and relevant form ten years from now. Avoid companies in industries where rapid technological disruption could render products obsolete.

strategies.name: Avoid High Innovation Risk Industries
strategies.description: Avoid industries where the risk of significant technological innovation is high, such as electronics and e-commerce. Prefer 'stodgier' industries like elevators and cosmetics where demand is durable and less susceptible to disruption.

strategies.name: Avoid Fast-Paced Innovation Industries
strategies.description: Industries with high innovation rates are especially unpredictable and unlikely to contain many quality companies. Avoid investing in industries prone to rapid innovation as they resemble lotteries with more losers than winners.

strategies.name: Sustained Profitability Filter
strategies.description: Favor companies with long track records of consistent profitability, as companies in rapidly innovating industries can see profitability collapse quickly (as illustrated by Nokia going from market leader to operating losses). Look for companies with many consecutive profitable years.

strategies.name: Stable Operating Margins
strategies.description: Prefer companies with stable and positive operating margins, avoiding those in industries where profit pools can rapidly shift due to innovation disruption.

strategies.name: Avoid Government-Dependent Revenue
strategies.description: Avoid companies where a large portion of revenue or profitability depends on government subsidies, contracts, or favorable regulation, as these can be changed unpredictably. Companies reliant on fixed infrastructure (telecoms, utilities, oil producers, miners) are particularly vulnerable to government impositions like carbon taxes or extraction excise taxes.

strategies.name: Avoid Customer Concentration Risk
strategies.description: Avoid companies that rely on a small number of customers for a significant portion of revenue. Concentrated customer bases confer bargaining power to those customers, leading to price concessions and economic degradation. Food and beverage companies selling primarily to large grocery retailers are cited as examples.

strategies.name: Avoid Supplier Concentration Risk
strategies.description: Avoid companies that depend on a small number of key suppliers or licensors, as loss of those relationships can be devastating. Safilo's loss of key brand licenses (Armani, Polo Ralph Lauren) to Luxottica caused its stock to lag the market by more than 80% over roughly a decade.

strategies.name: Avoid Value Chain Concentration
strategies.description: Avoid companies where any part of the value chain (customers, suppliers, distributors, employees, bankers, or even shareholders) is highly concentrated, as this harbors hidden risks that can degrade economics. The GDS example shows how intermediaries captured most of the airline industry's economic value.

strategies.name: Favor High Operating Margins (Low Dependency Indicator)
strategies.description: Companies with strong operating margins and high returns on capital, like Amadeus in the GDS space, tend to be the ones that control critical chokepoints in value chains rather than being dependent on them. High operating margins can indicate pricing power and low dependency on external stakeholders.

strategies.name: Avoid Subsidy-Driven Growth Companies
strategies.description: Be wary of companies showing explosive revenue growth driven by government subsidies or industrial assistance programs, as such growth is transient. The renewable energy boom-bust cycle in Europe (pre- and post-2008) illustrates how subsidy withdrawal can take companies from explosive growth to near bankruptcy.

strategies.name: Avoid Companies Vulnerable to New Entrants
strategies.description: Avoid companies whose competitive advantages are based primarily on legacy infrastructure or closed national markets rather than durable competitive moats, as free trade agreements and online commerce have made it much easier for new rivals to enter markets.

strategies.name: Avoid Fad-Driven Revenue Companies
strategies.description: Avoid companies whose revenue is concentrated in a single product or brand experiencing a sudden spike in popularity, as such fads typically collapse within a few years. Look for companies with diversified, stable revenue streams rather than those dependent on hype-driven products with no practical need.

strategies.name: Premium Pricing Power Filter
strategies.description: Favor companies with strong profit margins indicating premium pricing power derived from brand strength or other competitive advantages. High operating margins suggest the company can price products above alternatives, a key quality characteristic.

strategies.name: Durable Competitive Advantage - Consistent Profitability
strategies.description: Screen for companies that have been consistently profitable over a long period, indicating durable competitive advantages that are resistant to shifting customer preferences and good-enough competition.

strategies.name: Avoid Highly Leveraged Companies Vulnerable to Preference Shifts
strategies.description: Companies facing potential shifts in customer preferences should have strong balance sheets to weather disruption. Avoid companies with high debt-to-equity ratios that could face bankruptcy (like Coleco) if a product fad collapses.

strategies.name: Stable Revenue Growth Filter
strategies.description: Favor companies with moderate, sustainable revenue growth rather than explosive growth that may indicate a fad. Extremely high growth rates in consumer products may signal unsustainable demand that could collapse rapidly.

strategies.name: Long-Term Earnings Growth Stability
strategies.description: Screen for companies with positive long-term earnings growth over 10 years, indicating resilience against shifting customer preferences and competitive threats from good-enough goods.

strategies.name: Niche Market Premium Margin
strategies.description: Niche products are better protected against good-enough competition because rivals need scale. Companies with high margins in niche categories (indicated by high operating margins combined with moderate market cap) may have stronger moats.

strategies.name: Quality at Fair Price (Munger Principle)
strategies.description: Prefer buying great (high-quality) companies at fair prices rather than mediocre companies at cheap prices. This implies focusing on quality metrics first and accepting reasonable (not necessarily bargain) valuations.
strategies.expression: roe > 0.15 and profit_margin > 0.15 and pe < market_pe * 1.5

strategies.name: Avoid Passing on Quality Due to High Multiples
strategies.description: Quality companies may appear to have elevated earnings multiples (high P/E), but the chapter argues markets systematically undervalue quality companies. Do not automatically screen out high-quality companies solely because their P/E appears high relative to the market.

strategies.name: Long-Term Quality Focus - Profitability Consistency
strategies.description: A quality-focused strategy emphasizes companies with consistent long-term profitability, implying sustained earnings over many years rather than short-term performance.

strategies.name: Ignore Short-Term Macro Noise
strategies.description: Resist allowing prevailing macroeconomic conditions to have undue influence on stock selection. Focus on company-specific quality attributes rather than macro-driven momentum or sentiment indicators.

strategies.name: Market Undervaluation of Quality Screen
strategies.description: The chapter argues stock markets tend to undervalue quality companies. Screen for high-quality companies (high ROE, strong margins, low debt) that may still be reasonably priced relative to their intrinsic quality.
strategies.expression: roe > 0.20 and operating_margin > 0.15 and debt_to_equity < 0.5 and margin_of_safety > 0

strategies.name: Long-Term Compounding Return Target
strategies.description: Seek companies that can sustain approximately 10% annual returns over 25+ year periods through compounding, as the difference between 10% and 7% annual returns doubles the terminal value over 25 years.

strategies.name: Steady Earnings Growth with Dividend
strategies.description: Favor companies with long-term sustainable earnings growth averaging around 9% combined with a 2% dividend yield, producing an all-in return of approximately 11%, over less predictable companies touting rapid near-term growth.

strategies.name: Avoid Deeply Cyclical Low-Margin Companies
strategies.description: Avoid deeply cyclical sectors lacking strong steady returns on capital or with thin margins, as these are anathema to quality investors despite occasional short-term outperformance.

strategies.name: Strong Steady Returns on Capital
strategies.description: Quality companies should demonstrate strong and steady returns on capital, distinguishing them from cyclical businesses that lack consistent profitability.

strategies.name: Accept Apparently Expensive Valuations for Quality
strategies.description: Quality stocks will often appear expensive on traditional valuation metrics. Investors should not automatically screen out higher P/E stocks if the underlying business quality justifies the premium.

strategies.name: Avoid Top-Down Driven Purchases
strategies.description: Avoid buying stocks primarily based on macroeconomic themes (trade, inflation, currency) rather than bottom-up company and industry fundamentals. Top-down driven purchases lead to weak conviction and premature selling.

strategies.name: Avoid Turnaround / Next-Monday Optimism Stocks
strategies.description: Avoid investing in troubled firms or problem-riddled industries based on management promises of imminent recovery. Structurally challenged companies and industries are far more likely to stay troubled than to recover. Avoid companies with poor profitability track records banking on future improvement.

strategies.name: Avoid Commodity-Dependent Companies
strategies.description: Avoid companies whose investment thesis depends on commodity price movements, as these are external factors beyond management's control and inherently unpredictable.

strategies.name: Avoid Companies Dependent on External/Political Outcomes
strategies.description: Avoid stocks where the investment case depends on external factors beyond the company's control, such as drug approvals, legislative changes, or commodity prices. These introduce arbitrary unpredictability.

strategies.name: Avoid Organizationally Complex Companies
strategies.description: Avoid industrial conglomerates or diversified financial institutions where organizational complexity creates an illusion of predictability and degrades forecasting reliability.

strategies.name: Positive Earnings - Avoid Loss-Making Firms
strategies.description: Avoid companies currently losing money, as these are more likely to be turnaround candidates subject to next-Monday optimism bias.

strategies.name: Stable Earnings Growth Filter
strategies.description: Favor companies with demonstrated long-term earnings growth rather than those promising future recovery, reducing exposure to overconfidence and turnaround traps.

strategies.name: Profit Warning Red Flag
strategies.description: Companies issuing a large profit warning (causing a stock price drop exceeding 10%) have a one-third probability of issuing another, usually larger, profit warning within one year. This signals potential deep-seated problems and warrants full reevaluation or position reduction.

strategies.name: Declining Operating Margins Filter
strategies.description: Monitor for unexplained pressure on margins as an early warning sign of deterioration. A significant decline in operating margin (e.g., from 6% to 1% as in the Tesco case) signals loss of competitive advantage and operational problems.

strategies.name: Revenue Growth Materialization Check
strategies.description: When expected growth fails to materialize, it is an early warning sign of a company falling from grace. Companies where revenue growth has stalled or turned negative despite prior expectations of growth should be flagged for reevaluation.

strategies.name: Avoid Fallen Angels - Declining Profitability
strategies.description: A quality company showing declining profit margins and declining return on equity over time is a potential 'boiling frog' situation. Screen for companies where ROE has deteriorated significantly, suggesting the competitive advantage is eroding.

strategies.name: Price Decline from 52-Week High Warning
strategies.description: A stock price drop exceeding 10% from recent highs (used as the threshold for a 'large profit warning') can indicate deep-seated problems. This serves as a quantitative trigger for reevaluation of the investment thesis.

strategies.name: Capital Expenditure Escalation Warning
strategies.description: Gradual increases in capital expenditure without corresponding revenue or earnings growth can signal deterioration. Companies requiring ever-increasing capex to maintain their position may be losing competitive advantage, as seen with Tesco's overseas expansion draining capital without adequate returns.

strategies.name: Debt Accumulation from Expansion
strategies.description: Companies that take on excessive debt to fund aggressive expansion (like Tesco's international growth) risk deterioration. High debt-to-equity ratios can signal overextension and capital misallocation.

strategies.name: EPS Growth Deterioration Check
strategies.description: A string of small earnings disappointments often signals larger problems. Negative or stagnant EPS growth is an early warning of a once-great company in decline.

strategies.name: Quality First, Valuation Second
strategies.description: Prioritize companies with high quality characteristics (strong ROE, stable earnings, predictable cash flows) before considering valuation. Avoid buying solely because something appears cheap on traditional valuation metrics.

strategies.name: Avoid Cheap Low-Quality Stocks
strategies.description: Quality investing is 'allergic to bargains' unless fundamental bottom-up analysis supports the thesis. Avoid stocks that appear cheap but lack quality fundamentals - low profitability, weak margins, or inconsistent earnings.

strategies.name: Quality Companies Beating Estimates Filter
strategies.description: Quality companies tend to exceed earnings estimates and deliver realized growth above expected growth. Look for companies with consistent earnings growth that have historically beaten forecasts, proxied by actual EPS growth exceeding market averages.

strategies.name: Tolerate Higher P/E for Quality
strategies.description: Quality companies may trade around 20x earnings or higher and still be good investments if they have predictable, growing earnings. Do not automatically reject stocks with P/E around 20 if quality characteristics are present.
strategies.expression: pe <= 25 and pe > 0 and roe > 0.15 and earnings_growth_10y > 0.08

strategies.name: Earnings Predictability and Value Creation
strategies.description: Markets tend to under-price the combination of predictability and value creation that quality companies offer. Screen for companies with consistent long-term profitability and strong returns on equity as proxies for predictability.

strategies.name: Long-Term Earnings Growth Driver
strategies.description: While short-term stock moves are driven ~80% by multiple changes, long-term returns are driven by earnings growth. Focus on companies with strong and sustained earnings growth over 10 years rather than short-term multiple expansion.

strategies.name: Anti-Mean-Reversion Quality Screen
strategies.description: Markets assume mean reversion in growth and returns, but well-positioned quality companies can sustain above-average performance. Screen for companies that have maintained high ROE and margins over long periods, suggesting mean reversion may not apply.
strategies.expression: roe > 0.20 and operating_margin > 0.15 and profitable_years >= 10 and earnings_growth_10y > 0.05

strategies.name: Quality at Reasonable Price (QARP)
strategies.description: Buy quality companies when valuations are attractive enough, even if multiples seem high in absolute terms. The combination of strong fundamentals with a P/E not excessively above market average represents an opportunity given systematic under-pricing of quality.
strategies.expression: pe > 0 and pe < 30 and roe > 0.20 and profit_margin > 0.10 and eps_growth_rate > 0.05

strategies.name: Sustainably High Return on Capital
strategies.description: Quality companies achieve strong returns on capital over the long term, indicating durable competitive advantages that overcome mean reversion.

strategies.name: Sustainable Revenue Growth
strategies.description: Quality companies deliver sustainably higher growth than average, not just recent sales growth but durable long-term growth.

strategies.name: Strong Operating Margins
strategies.description: Companies with durable economics and competitive advantages typically exhibit strong operating margins, reflecting attractive industry structures and unique competitive positioning.

strategies.name: Sustained High CFROI (eCAP) Filter
strategies.description: Identify companies that have sustained a cash flow return on investment (CFROI) above 8% for at least five consecutive years, representing companies with a sustained competitive advantage period (eCAP). These represent approximately 12% of the European index and are overweight Consumer and Healthcare sectors.

strategies.name: Quality EPS Growth vs Market
strategies.description: Screen for companies delivering earnings growth significantly above the market average. The AKO long book companies delivered an EPS CAGR of 10% versus the European market's CAGR of just over 1% over 2009-2014.

strategies.name: Avoid Roll-Up Acquisition Strategies
strategies.description: Avoid companies pursuing aggressive roll-up acquisition strategies, as 70% of deals fail to hit top-line synergy targets and merging companies lose 2-5% of combined customers. Companies with high debt from acquisitions and no organic growth template should be screened out.

strategies.name: Earnings Predictability Filter
strategies.description: The market does not pay a premium for predictability/safety of companies, suggesting mispricing opportunity. Screen for companies with consistent earnings that the market undervalues on a safety basis — companies with many consecutive profitable years.

strategies.name: High Market Share / Dominant Position
strategies.description: Favor companies with dominant market share positions (e.g., Ambev's ~68% market share in Brazilian beer). Such companies tend to have pricing power and sustainable competitive advantages.
